paper_title: Free Lunch for Pass@$k$? Low Cost Diverse Sampling for Diffusion Language Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Diverse outputs in text generation are necessary for effective exploration in complex reasoning tasks, such as code generation and mathematical problem solving. Such Pass@$k$ problems benefit from distinct candidates covering the solution space. However, traditional sampling approaches often waste computational resources on repetitive failure modes. While Diffusion Language Models have emerged as a competitive alternative to the prevailing Autoregressive paradigm, they remain susceptible to this redundancy, with independent samples frequently collapsing into similar modes. To address this, we propose a training free, low cost intervention to enhance generative diversity in Diffusion Language Models. Our approach modifies intermediate samples in a batch sequentially, where each sample is repelled from the feature space of previous samples, actively penalising redundancy. Unlike prior methods that require retraining or beam search, our strategy incurs negligible computational overhead, while ensuring that each sample contributes a unique perspective to the batch. We evaluate our method on the HumanEval and GSM8K benchmarks using the LLaDA-8B-Instruct model. Our results demonstrate significantly improved diversity and Pass@$k$ performance across various temperature settings. As a simple modification to the sampling process, our method offers an immediate, low-cost improvement for current and future Diffusion Language Models in tasks that benefit from diverse solution search. We make our code available at https://github.com/sean-lamont/odd.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 5 ] . [ 1 3 9 8 4 0 . 3 0 6 2 : r Free Lunch for Pass@k? Low Cost Diverse Sampling for Diffusion Language Models Sean Lamont1,2, Christian Walder3, Paul Montague2, Amir Dezfouli4, Michael Norrish1 1Australian National University 2Defence Science and Technology Group 3Google DeepMind 4BIMLOGIQ sean.lamont@anu.edu.au

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Diverse outputs in text generation are necessary for effective exploration in complex reasoning tasks, such as code generation and mathematical problem solving. Such Pass@k problems benefit from distinct candidates covering the solution space. However, traditional sampling approaches often waste computational resources on repetitive failure modes. While Diffusion Language Models have emerged as competitive alternative to the prevailing Autoregressive paradigm, they remain susceptible to this redundancy, with independent samples frequently collapsing into similar modes. To address this, we propose training free, low cost intervention to enhance generative diversity in Diffusion Language Models. Our approach modifies intermediate samples in batch sequentially, where each sample is repelled from the feature space of previous samples, actively penalising redundancy. Unlike prior methods that require retraining or beam search, our strategy incurs negligible computational overhead, while ensuring that each sample contributes unique perspective to the batch. We evaluate our method on the HumanEval and GSM8K benchmarks using the LLaDA-8B-Instruct model. Our results demonstrate significantly improved diversity and Pass@k performance across various temperature settings. As simple modification to the sampling process, our method offers an immediate, low-cost improvement for current and future Diffusion Language Models in tasks that benefit from diverse solution search. We make our code available at https://github.com/sean-lamont/odd.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) such as LLaDA [13] have recently demonstrated performance comparable to Autoregressive (AR) models while offering distinct advantages, including faster parallel generation, superior constraint adherence [8] and flexible generation controls that allow users to trade off speed and quality. Despite these capabilities, persistent challenge in generation for both paradigms is mode collapse or redundancy. When sampling multiple solutions, standard techniques such as temperature scaling or beam search often produce highly correlated outputs. This redundancy diminishes the utility of multiple samples in Pass@k tasks such as coding or mathematics, as they result in repetitive outputs which do not effectively explore the possible solution space. Diversity has recently become an active topic in Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training for Language Models [15, 7] where it is often prerequisite for learning from sparse rewards. This is particularly critical in domains like competition mathematics, where correct solutions are rare. In such settings, sampling strategy that collapses to single mode may never generate correct trajectory, even if the solution lies within the base models capabilities. By improving diverse sampling to maximize Pass@k, we increase the likelihood of discovering rare successful paths, thereby providing Figure 1: Example outputs from LLaDA with standard sampling (left) and our approach (right), both with temperature of 1. For this example, the base sampler achieved Pass@16 of 0, while our model found 3 valid solutions. Despite having the same temperature, standard sampling leads to mode collapse over an incorrect approach, whereas our method optimises to select samples distinct from those previously explored. the valuable reward signal needed to drive optimisation. To date, the primary research focus has been on post-training interventions incorporating diversity directly into the optimisation objective [7, 15]. Since the initial generation forms the basis of what is selected for downstream tasks or training, effective inference-time diversity remains an orthogonal and complementary objective. Prior work in inference-time diversity has been restricted to AR models, focusing largely on diverse variants of beam search [16, 6, 10, 14]. These approaches are restricted to AR decoding and often require training separate value models or incur significant latency penalties due to complex beam management [14]. Unlike AR models, diffusion models possess global view of the sample at every inference step. This allows for methods which leverage both the current context and the models predictions for the remaining tokens. This has been exploited in the image and protein synthesis domains, where sample trajectories are modified to optimise batch-wide diversity term. Until now, this has not been explored in the context of text diffusion models [3, 11]. To address these limitations we propose ODD: Orthogonal Diverse Diffusion, which encourages diverse (odd) outputs to be sampled during inference. We use the intermediate states of the diffusion process to generate feature vectors, for which we apply lightweight repulsion term during inference. As we generate batch of samples, the logits for the i-th sample are actively pushed away from the subspace spanned by samples {1, . . . , 1}. This ensures that the additional computation needed for larger batches effectively contributes to the exploration of solution space. We validate our approach on HumanEval and GSM8K, demonstrating that we can boost Pass@k with minimal overhead. To summarise our contributions: We present training free framework for improving generative diversity in DLMs with minimal time and space overhead. We use this to introduce diversity improving loss based on maximising the component of the current sample orthogonal to previous samples. Testing our approach over HumanEval and subset of GSM8K, we demonstrate significant and consistent improvements across various temperature settings. We open source our code. This can be used for immediate improvements to Pass@k for DLMs, while our codebase allows for easy experimentation and evaluation over different feature extractors, diversity terms and benchmarks to help encourage further improvements in the area. In addition, we release all experiment logs and data for full transparency and reproducibility, available at https://sean-lamont.github.io/odd/. 2 Figure 2: An overview of our proposed diverse sampling framework, using our orthogonal diversity loss. Given full batch of samples, feature vectors (xi) are extracted for the logits xi for each sample {1, . . . , n}. We then loop through each sample, compute an orthogonal basis for all previous sample features B<i, and calculate the projection of the current sample features onto this basis. We then take the norm of the residual with respect to this projection as the diversity objective. Optimising this then moves the sample in direction diverse compared to previous samples. 1.1 Related Work Recent works have integrated diversity directly into RL post-training [15, 7]. By improving inferencetime diversity, our method is complementary and applicable to any pre-trained diffusion model without modification. Several inference-time algorithms have been proposed for the AR domain. Diverse variants of beam search [10, 16, 6] have been effective, though can incur significant computational overhead [14]. Other strategies involve conditioning the model on diverse prompts or random seeds, but do not address redundancy over multiple attempts as they treat samples independently [12]. Other work has tried to explicitly condition on past attempts to force exploration, but require previously generated output before enforcing diversity [9]. Similarly, recent approaches which filter diverse sets from larger pool are limited by the overhead of generating the initial candidates [15, 5]. In contrast, we enforce diversity during the generation process itself, with minimal overhead. In the context of Diffusion Models, inference-time diversity has been explored for image and protein synthesis [11, 3]. DiverseFlow [11] and similar methods [3] modify the sampling trajectory by optimising batch-wide diversity objective. As part of our work we extend DiverseFlow [11] to the text domain (Section 3.2), where we find improvements upon standard sampling. We then demonstrate significant additional improvements using our novel ODD algorithm. For text diffusion, recent work has attempted to promote diversity by adding noise to embeddings [17]. By treating samples independently, it does not effectively account for what has already been explored in given batch. Our approach explicitly models the dependency between samples, sequentially projecting each new sample away from the subspace spanned by previous generations. This ensures structured exploration rather than random variance.

sections.title: 2 Methodology
sections.content: 2.1 Diffusion Language Models Unlike AR models which generate tokens sequentially, DLMs such as LLaDA generate all tokens in sequence simultaneously through an iterative refinement process [13]. Formally, we define forward process that gradually corrupts clean data sample x0 RSV (where is sequence length and is vocabulary size) into fully masked state xT over timesteps. This corruption is modeled as masking process, where each token is independently replaced by special [MASK] token with probability determined by time-dependent schedule γ(t). Specifically, the forward transition 3 probability for token at sequence position j, denoted xt is defined as: q(xt jx0 ) = 1 γ(t) γ(t) 0 j, given the clean token x0 = x0 = [MASK] if xt if xt otherwise (1) where γ(t) is monotonically increasing function such that γ(0) = 0 and γ(T ) = 1. The generative reverse process then aims to learn denoising network pθ(xt1xt) to recover the original tokens from their masked initial states. Several models including LLaDA employ re-masking strategy, where subset of the high-confidence predictions are kept, with the rest re-masked for the next iteration [13]. This global view of the sequence at every step captures bidirectional relationships and, in the context of this work, enables inference-time interventions on the entire remaining sequence. 2.2 Inference-Time Diversity Sampling For batch of size with given sample {1, . . . , B}, each step in the diffusion process RBSV . We present simple inferenceyields predicted logits which we denote with xt time framework for modifying the predicted logits to penalise redundancy. Our method is applied independently for each time step, so we omit the and use xi to refer to the (S )dimensional logits for sample with assumed timestep t. We assume sample with logits xi, previous samples x<i = [x1; ; xi1], and feature extractor : Rd. For simplicity, we denote the feature vector for the current state as vi (xi), and the concatenation of feature vectors from previous states as v<i [F (x1); ; (xi1)]. We define our diversity term as Ldiv(vi, v<i), which can be any differentiable loss function capturing diversity of features. For each sample i, our framework returns updated logits ˆxi which are pushed to optimise the diversity term with respect to previous samples in the batch: ˆxi = xi α xiLdiv(vi, v<i) where α is hyperparameter controlling the strength of the repulsion. In line with previous work [11], we anneal α linearly with throughout the diffusion process. Intuitively, this gives higher diversity earlier in the generation when the high level structure is being formed, and prevents excessive intervention for later steps where finer details are commonly filled in. (2) 2.3 Feature Extraction and Quality Scoring The effectiveness of diversity intervention will depend on the choice of feature extractor , which must encode the semantic or structural qualities of the generation we wish to diversify. Several prior works have used computationally intensive features, such as pre-trained semantic encoders [14] or learned similarity classifiers [7]. To ensure negligible overhead, we instead opt for lightweight feature extraction strategy that operates directly on the models output distribution. For sample at timestep t, we construct unified probability distribution Pi RSV that captures both the models uncertainty and its committed decisions. For indices corresponding to currently masked tokens, we compute the softmax over the predicted logits: Pi,s = softmax(xi,s). However, for unmasked tokens which have already materialised, we assign probability of 1 to the generated token index and 0 elsewhere. We then obtain fixed-size representation vi RV by applying max-pooling over the sequence dimension S: vi = max sS This vector vi represents global confidence profile of the sequence across the vocabulary. By setting realised tokens to 1, we ensure that the feature vector is weighted combination of the sequences established history and its intended future trajectory. Pi,s. (3) Quality Awareness Strictly enforcing diversity can push the model towards low-probability, incoherent modes. To mitigate this, we also include use scalar quality score qi as part of the feature extractor. We define qi as the average maximum confidence of the unmasked tokens in the sequence: (cid:88) (4) qi = 1 Ui max Pi,s,v sUi 4 Algorithm 1: ODD: Orthogonal Diverse Diffusion Input : Model logits = [x1, . . . , xn], step size α, diffusion timestep t, feature extractor // = [v1, . . . , vn], = [q1, . . . , qn] // sg() stops gradient tracking Output :Updated logits // Compute features and qualities V, (X) ; // Initialise orthonormal basis with the first token (cid:16) v1 (cid:17) ; v12 ) α b1 sg B<i [b1] αt (1 1 Ltotal 0 for in {2, . . . , n} do (vi) pi projB<i // Accumulate loss to capture diversity with previous samples L(i) orth qi vi sg(pi)2 Ltotal Ltotal + L(i) orth // Update orthonormal basis ri sg(vi pi) bi ri ri2 B<i [B<i : bi] // Update logits simultaneously αt Ltotal return where Ui is the set of unmasked token indices for sample i. By weighting with qi, the model is discouraged from excessive diversity in areas it is confident in, preserving generation quality. 2.3.1 Orthogonal Diverse Diffusion Algorithm 1 outlines our ODD approach. For the i-th sample in the batch with feature vector vi, we maintain an orthogonal basis for the history vectors v<i. This is done using the Gram-Schmidt algorithm, where we define B<i [b1, . . . , bi1] as the orthogonal basis for the history. We then set the diversity term Ldiv to be the negative norm of the projection of the residual of current sample features vi onto the previous samples subspace B<i, scaled by the samples quality score qi: Lorth(vi, v<i) qi (cid:16) vi projB<i (vi) (cid:17) . (5) The gradient Lorth directs the logits to minimise the magnitude of this projection, effectively forcing the model to explore the null space of previous generations. In combination with lightweight feature extractor , this approach is extremely cheap as we are only tracking the gradients with respect to the model logits after generation. As outlined in Algorithm 1, we apply stop-gradients to the projection operations to treat the established subspace as fixed target, restricting backpropagation strictly to the active token and preventing the formation of an expensive, recursive computation graph. Unlike the optimisation objective of DiverseFlow [11] which optimises the batch globally, our method employs greedy, sequential projection that forces each sample to independently diverge only from its predecessors. direct consequence of this formulation is that the generation trajectory of any sample depends exclusively on the subspace spanned by earlier samples. Therefore the resulting output for sample will be batch size invariant, giving the same output across runs (given the same logits from the base model) regardless of the total batch size k, for any i. 5 Step Size (α) Temperature (θ) 0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2. Bold values indicate the best result for each θ. LLaDA + DPP (Algorithm 2) uses global DPP objective [11]. LLaDA + ODD uses our approach in Algorithm 1.

sections.title: 3 Experiments
sections.content: 3.1 Experimental Setup We evaluate our approach using the LLaDA-8B-Instruct model [13], which we quantise to 4-bit for our experiments. We compare three setups. The first is standard LLaDA with no logit intervention. For the second approach, we update logits using the DPP based loss from [11] as our objective Ldiv from Equation 2 (Outlined in Algorithm 2). This tests our diversity framework with global batch diversity objective, which has seen success in image diffusion. Finally, we test our ODD approach as outlined in Algorithm 1, where we use the loss in Equation 5 as Ldiv. We conduct experiments on two standard benchmarks, HumanEval [1] and GSM8K [2]. We take the first 200 problems in GSM8K to facilitate large scale analysis of our approach over varying temperature and alpha levels,

sections.title: HUMANEVAL
sections.content: 22.5 0.3 23.8 0.5 30.8 0.4 32.0 0.2 +3.9% Overhead (%) +5.8% Table 2: Wall time comparison for generation between standard LLaDA (Baseline) and ODD (Batch Size 16). We report the execution time per batch (in seconds) averaged over all α and temperature settings for each approach, and the relative overhead percentage. Our method introduces minimal latency across both benchmarks, and scales independently of model size. with 8 runs per configuration to improve confidence in the results. We compare approaches across grid of temperature settings, as well as α values for ODD to investigate the influence of the step size on the final results. We fix the number of diffusion steps to 32, with generative length of 64. The batch size is fixed to 16 and we report the empirical Pass@16, which is the percentage of outputs which found the correct answer in at least one of the 16 attempts. 3.2 Results Table 1 presents our main results. We observe consistent and significant improvement in our approach across both benchmark tasks. As expected, the improvements are particularly large for temperature θ = 0.0, which corresponds to the greedy decoding regime where Pass@k = Pass@1. For θ = 2, the improvements are especially noteworthy in the HumanEval benchmark. For HumanEval, the generated output must correctly compile and pass test cases, and so requires both correct syntax and reasoning for the complete output. Excessive diversity at the token level, which we observe at θ = 2, therefore results in significantly reduced performance for the baseline. As our approach optimises both diversity and quality, it provides mechanism to correct for this without tuning. For GSM8K, in all cases, we observe consistent increase in performance as we increase our step size α. The improvements in HumanEval are significant, however we observe large α values decrease performance. Given the strict correctness requirements discussed above, this likely corresponds to the inflection point of the diversity-quality tradeoff. The final key observation is that our approach is far less sensitive to temperature than baseline, which gives confidence that our approach provides improvements with minimal tuning. Interestingly, while the DPP objective from DiverseFlow [11] globally optimises across all samples, our experiments demonstrate that our greedy, stop-gradient approach results in improved Pass@k. One possible explanation is that since joint optimisation causes all samples to repel each other, this may push better, high probability samples off the optimal mode to achieve diversity. Another possible disadvantage is the complex dependencies between all samples in batch, which may create chaotic optimisation trajectories when optimised jointly. In contrast, our objective is simply the projection onto the detached subspace of previous samples, which is static hyperplane. Standard sampling treats every generation independently, with no shared information between attempts. Consequently, increasing the batch size often results in wasted compute, as the model repeatedly samples the most likely modes. In contrast, ODD introduces batch-aware dependency that explicitly trades off exploitation (quality) and exploration (diversity). While individual sample quality (Pass@1) can slightly decrease as we force the model to explore less probable paths (see Figure 4), the coverage of the solution space (Pass@16) increases significantly. This confirms that our method effectively converts compute into useful exploration, ensuring that each additional sample contributes unique perspective to the batch rather than repeating previous failure modes. 3.2.1 Overhead Analysis Table 2 shows the average time overhead between ODD and LLaDA with standard sampling, for the experiments in Section 3.2. We observe only small increase for both benchmarks, particularly considering the rate of improvement in Pass@16. As our approach only optimises logits after they are computed, the overhead is independent of the base model, and so will have better relative overhead as model size increases. Furthermore, the operations used by ODD are lightweight in comparison to the model forward pass. We conduct more detailed overhead analysis in Appendix E.

sections.title: 4 Conclusion and Future Work
sections.content: We presented ODD, training free inference intervention that significantly enhances the diversity and Pass@k performance of Diffusion Language Models. By sequentially projecting intermediate logits away from the subspace of previous samples, we effectively minimise sampling redundancy at negligible computational cost. Our results on HumanEval and GSM8K demonstrate that this simple geometric repulsion enables more effective exploration of the solution space, particularly in domains requiring complex reasoning where valid solutions are sparse. Our work highlights unique advantage of the diffusion paradigm, which is the ability to intervene globally on the generation process. Unlike Autoregressive models, diffusion models allow for global sequence optimisation, enabling low cost improvements to sample efficiency. With inference time compute becoming major factor in scaling reasoning capabilities, our approach helps improve the efficiency of resources during generation. 4.1 Future Work Future work should explore the effectiveness of different feature extractors. There are possible improvements to be made by considering, for example, the positional information in tokens. The semantics of partial sequences could be captured, as has been done in AR models (e.g. [14, 7]), although the overhead could be limiting factor. Dimensionality reduction of the feature space could also be explored to further reduce the overhead of our approach.

sections.title: A Cumulative Results
sections.content: Table 3 presents the cumulative problem coverage, calculated as the union of all successfully solved problems across all runs in Section 3.2 for each temperature regime (5 temperature settings with 8 runs per benchmark, for Pass@16 gives us 640 samples per problem). Because ODD generates dependent samples, this metric better reflects the bounds of the reachable solution space rather than standard independent Pass@k scaling, which we observe in Figure 5. On HumanEval, where strict syntactic and logical constraints make solutions sparse, baseline sampling covers only 110 problems (67.1%). ODD (α = 16) expands this to 129 problems (78.7%), representing significant 17.3% relative improvement. Even for the cases where α > 16, there remains significant improvement in cumulative problems solved for these suboptimal α, where Pass@16 performance significantly drops. This demonstrates ODDs strength as an exploration mechanism even for poor choices of α, where it is able to discover valid, rare reasoning paths that standard sampling misses entirely, even after 640 trials over various temperatures. In contrast, the baseline effectively saturates the 200-problem GSM8K subset (198 problems, 99.0% coverage). Unlike HumanEvals strict execution constraints, GSM8K evaluates only the final numerical answer. Given large compute budget across high temperatures, standard sampling acts as an unconstrained random walk which can occasionally find the correct final answer regardless of reasoning. ODD instead enforces structured exploration over distinct generation trajectories. While this slightly reduces the absolute coverage (192-197 problems), it dramatically streamlines the search. Consequently, ODD significantly improves practical sample efficiency  (Table 1)  , raising Pass@16 to reliably find more solutions within more practical inference budget.

sections.title: B Diversity Dynamics
sections.content: In Figure 3, we analyse the semantic diversity of the generated batches using the average cosine similarity between sentence embeddings. We observe an interesting dynamic behavior in ODD depending on the temperature regime: Low Temperature (θ 1.0): The baseline exhibits high similarity, with mode collapse in lower temperatures. ODD significantly reduces similarity, forcing the model to explore diverse paths even when the underlying probability distribution is sharp. High Temperature (θ 1.5): The baseline naturally produces diverse but often incoherent outputs (lower similarity). Interestingly, ODD tends to reduce diversity compared to the baseline in this regime. This suggests our quality-weighted repulsion acts as filter, guiding the model back towards high-confidence regions when the noise level is excessive. 10 Figure 3: Average batch diversity (1 - cosine similarity of sentence embeddings) for GSM8K (top) and HumanEval (bottom), comparing ODD to the baseline (α = 0, as indicated by dashed lines). ODD increases diversity at low temperatures and acts as coherence filter at high temperatures (evidenced by the improved Pass@16 in Table 1), effectively balancing exploration and quality. This demonstrates that ODD naturally trades off diversity and quality, by adaptively promoting diversity when needed and also promoting coherence when diversity is already sufficient. Pareto Efficiency and Trade-offs Figure 4: Pass@1 vs Pass@16 Pareto frontiers. α = 0 represents standard LLaDA sampling (baseline). Data points represent decreasing temperatures from right (θ = 0) to left (θ = 2). Top (GSM8K): ODD trades off individual accuracy (shifting left) for superior batch coverage (shifting up). Bottom (HumanEval): For α 16, ODD achieves Pareto improvement, boosting coverage without loss of quality. Figure 4 visualises the efficiency of our sampling strategy by plotting individual sample quality (Pass@1, as determined by the average correctness in batch) against batch coverage (Pass@16). HumanEval (Pareto Improvement): For code generation, moderate repulsion (α 16) yields vertical lift over the baseline. We achieve significantly higher coverage (Pass@16) without degrading the quality of individual samples (Pass@1). This indicates that ODD provides Pareto improvement over baseline for lower alpha values. GSM8K (Exploration Trade-off): For reasoning tasks, we observe shift up and to the left. To maximise batch coverage (Pass@16), ODD sacrifices some individual sample accuracy (Pass@1). This confirms that the model is being forced to explore less probable and riskier reasoning paths. However, the net result is higher probability of finding the correct answer within single batch, capability the baseline cannot match even at high temperatures. Empirical Pass@k Figure 5: Empirical Pass@k for ODD over GSM8K (top) and HumanEval (bottom). We observe consistent improvement relative to baseline for ODD. Given the batch size invariance of ODD, we expect this trend would continue for higher batch sizes. Figure 5 illustrates the empirical Pass@k scaling behavior of ODD compared to standard baseline sampling across both the GSM8K and HumanEval benchmarks. Empirical Pass@k measures the probability that at least one correct solution is found within generated subset of samples, where we take the first out of the 16 samples from our experiments in Section 3.2. As observed in the baseline curves, standard independent sampling suffers from severe diminishing returns as scales. Because the samples are generated independently, the model frequently collapses 13 into identical failure modes, causing the curves to plateau early. In contrast, ODD maintains significantly steeper trajectory as increases. By explicitly penalising redundancy and forcing the model into orthogonal subspaces, ODD ensures that every additional unit of compute (each subsequent sample up to = 16) explores genuinely novel reasoning path or syntactic structure. Given the batch size invariance of ODD, the curves shown for 16 are identical to the prefix of the curve for any larger batch size > 16. This predictable scaling property suggests that ODD will continue to efficiently expand the explored solution space as compute budgets increase.

sections.title: E DiverseFlow Baseline
sections.content: Algorithm 2: Joint Batch Optimisation (DiverseFlow [11]) Input : Model logits = [x1, . . . , xn], step size α, diffusion timestep t, feature extractor , numerical jitter ϵ Output :Updated logits // Compute normalised features and qualities V, (X) ; αt (1 1 ) α // Construct the DPP L-ensemble matrix ; Qmat QQ ; Qmat ; // Compute the negative log-likelihood of diversity log det(L + ϵI) log det (cid:0)L + (1 + ϵ)I(cid:1)(cid:17) Ljoint // Update all logits simultaneously via full backprop αt LDPP return (cid:16) // Rnd, Rn // Feature similarity kernel // Quality outer product, Qmat[i, j] = qiqj // Element-wise scaling To evaluate our approach against previous global diversity methods, we adapt the global optimisation strategy of DiverseFlow [11], originally proposed for continuous image diffusion. The methodology is detailed in Algorithm 2. This baseline models the diversity of the generated batch using Determinantal Point Process [4] kernel. Given the extracted feature vectors and quality scores derived from the model logits, the algorithm constructs an L-ensemble Gram matrix L. This matrix is formed via the elementwise multiplication of the pure feature similarity kernel (K = ) and the quality outer product (Qmat), weighting the pairwise similarities by the samples respective confidence scores. The objective LDPP then minimises the negative log-determinant of (stabilised by numerical jitter ϵ), which geometrically corresponds to maximising the n-dimensional volume enclosed by the batch feature vectors. 14 Figure 6: Computational overhead of ODD compared to standard sampling. Top: Global averages across all runs. Bottom: Scaling behavior demonstrating time and memory overheads across batch size (B), sequence length (S), and diffusion steps (T ). ODD incurs less than 10% relative time penalty, with memory scaling independently of the base model size.

sections.title: F Computational Overhead
sections.content: To comprehensively evaluate the computational overhead of ODD, we profiled generation using the LLaDA-8B-Instruct model in both standard BF16 and 4-bit quantised precision. We measured latency and VRAM footprint scaling across batch size (B), generated sequence length (S), and diffusion steps (T ), with 24 configurations for each problem in HumanEval and the 200 problem subset of GSM8K. The results are presented in Figure 6. Overall, ODD incurs minimal latency, effectively acting as low cost intervention for scaling Pass@k. Across all configurations, the time overhead consistently remains below 10%. Importantly, the absolute memory and compute introduced by ODD are entirely independent of the base model. Although our approach is model agnostic, the relative latency penalty is slightly higher for the BF16 model. This corresponds to the speed decrease from quantisation overheads, with the base model being approximately 2.4s slower in the quantised setting. As expected, the relative memory overhead for the larger BF16 model is below that of the quantised model. We observe distinct scaling dynamics across the generation parameters. Because ODD sequentially tracks the history of the batch, its memory overhead scales at O(B2) with batch size (B). The VRAM penalty remains negligible (5 15%) while time overhead remains flat. Scaling the generated sequence length (S) results in linear increase in both time and memory overhead. This is driven by the requirements of maintaining and backpropagating through the graph during the feature extraction phase. Despite this linear scaling, the peak overhead at = 128 remains highly manageable. It could be further alleviated by taking subset of logits (e.g. top-k) rather than the whole vocabulary for each token. Finally, the relative time and memory overheads are invariant to the number of diffusion steps (T ). Because the intervention is applied locally at each denoising timestep, its computational cost scales in proportion to the forward pass of the base model.
